perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party therefore agrees that neither it nor its Representatives will at any time make any independent business or personal use of, retain, copy, divulge, disclose, reveal or communicate to any other person or organization (except as expressly authorized in writing by Originating Party, as required to analyze the Business R elationship or as required to fulfill Receiving Party’s obligations to Originating Party) any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party therefore agrees that neither it nor its Representatives will endeavor to avoid making independent business or personal use of, retain, copy, divulge, disclose, reveal or communicate to any other person or organization (except as expressly authorized in writing by Originating Party, as required to analyze the Business R elationship or as required to fulfill Receiving Party’s obligations to Originating Party) any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will at any time make any independent business or personal use of' to 'will endeavor to avoid making' significantly weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor to avoid' implies an attempt, but not a guarantee, and does not constitute a strict prohibition. This creates ambiguity and makes the obligation difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri Revised Statutes § 417.453 protects trade secrets. While not directly contradicting this statute, weakening the non-disclosure obligation to 'endeavor to avoid' undermines the protection of confidential information and trade secrets, making it more difficult to prove misappropriation, which is a violation of the spirit and intent of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 417.453 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVI TRADE AND COMMERCE Chapter 417 < > • Effective - 28 Aug 1995 417.453. Definitions. — As used in sections 417.450 to 417.467 , the following terms mean: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" : (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of a person by another person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of a person without express or implied consent by another person who: a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. Before a material change of position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake; or c. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) "Person" , a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity, whether for profit or not for profit; (4) "Trade secret" , information, including but not limited to, technical or nontechnical data, a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by p
perturbation.justification: The change from "will at any time make any independent business or personal use of" to "will endeavor to avoid making" weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially undermining trade secret protection. Missouri Revised Statutes § 417.453 defines "Misappropriation" as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of a person without express or implied consent'. By changing the language to "endeavor to avoid," the contract introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation, making it more difficult to prove misappropriation, which contradicts the intent of the law to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall not reverse -engineer, analyze, attempt to duplicate or otherwise attempt to determine the design or method of compiling samples, prototypes or products of the Originating Party except pursuant to a mutually acceptable, written agreement executed by t he parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall, when feasible, avoid reverse-engineering, analyzing, attempting to duplicate or otherwise attempting to determine the design or method of compiling samples, prototypes or products of the Originating Party except pursuant to a mutually acceptable, written agreement executed by t he parties.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'when feasible' introduces ambiguity. It's unclear what determines feasibility. This creates a loophole where the Receiving Party could claim it wasn't feasible to avoid reverse engineering, weakening the protection of the Originating Party's intellectual property.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 defines trade secrets and prohibits their misappropriation. By adding 'when feasible', the contract introduces a subjective element, making it more difficult for the Originating Party to prove that the Receiving Party intentionally misappropriated trade secrets through reverse engineering, thereby undermining the protection offered by the federal statute.
perturbation.location: IV.NO REVERSE ENGINEERI NG; WORK PRODUCT :
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces the term 'when feasible,' creating an ambiguous exception to the original obligation not to reverse-engineer. This ambiguity weakens the protection of the Originating Party's intellectual property, as the Receiving Party could argue it was not 'feasible' to avoid reverse engineering. The Economic Espionage Act of 1996, as referenced by snippet 1, aims to protect trade secrets. By introducing the subjective term 'when feasible', the modified contract makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation, thus contradicting the intent of the law to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PRO VIDED UNDER THIS AGREEMENT “AS IS” WITHOUT WARRANTIES OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, AND DISCLOSER DISCLAIMS ANY IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NON -INFRINGEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PRO VIDED UNDER THIS AGREEMENT “GENERALLY AS IS” WITHOUT WARRANTIES OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, AND DISCLOSER DISCLAIMS ANY IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NON -INFRINGEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the word 'generally' before 'AS IS' introduces ambiguity regarding the extent of the disclaimer of warranties. It suggests that under certain unspecified circumstances, some warranties might apply, contradicting the clear and absolute 'AS IS' provision. This makes the disclaimer less certain and open to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 governs the exclusion or modification of warranties. The 'AS IS' clause is a recognized way to disclaim warranties. By adding 'generally,' the clause becomes ambiguous and can be interpreted as not being a complete disclaimer, potentially negating the effect of the 'AS IS' clause and conflicting with the intention to fully disclaim warranties under the UCC.
perturbation.location: VI.NO COMMITMENT; NO LI CENSE; NO WARRANTY :
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity into the 'AS IS' disclaimer by adding the word 'generally'. This weakens the disclaimer, creating a potential contradiction with UCC § 2-316, which recognizes 'AS IS' as a clear exclusion of warranties. The law snippet states that 'all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty'. By adding 'generally', the contract creates ambiguity and fails to make plain that there is no implied warranty, leading to a legal contradiction.
